one_line_takeaway: TIP shows that a black-box, tree-search-optimized attacker LLM can generate MCP tool-response payloads that are simultaneously near-perfectly effective (up to 100% ASR), an order of magnitude cheaper to find than prior tree-of-attacks methods, and stealthy enough (high cosine similarity to benign outputs) to survive perplexity filters, finetuned detectors, and prompt-sandwiching defenses -- including against GPT-4o in a live VS Code MCP deployment.
key_finding: No-Defense setting (Table I): TIP reaches 100.0% ASR on GetProduct, GetWeather, and ExpediaBooking, and 95.0% on ShipManager (where Fixed scores 0.0% and TAP scores 0.0%). TIP needs only 100 queries on GetProduct vs. TAP's 2580 (and similarly ~20-320 queries elsewhere vs. TAP's ~2480-2580), an order-of-magnitude reduction the paper attributes to path-aware feedback. Under defenses, TIP retains substantial ASR where TAP and Fixed largely collapse: under Sandwich Prevention (Fraud/Product) TIP holds 100.0% ASR vs. Fixed's 5.0%; under Finetuned Detector, TIP reaches 97.8% ASR on ExpediaBooking (a task where TAP scores only 20.0%); under Perplexity Filtering TIP reaches 100.0% ASR on GetProduct/GetWeather versus TAP's 52.5%/87.0%. Transferability (Table II): payloads optimized on the small training ensemble reach up to 100% ASR against the much larger Llama3.3-70B-Instruct on GetWeather (No Defense), and 92.0% ASR on Llama3.3-70B under Instruction Prevention in the Data-Steal setting -- versus corresponding TAP figures of 0.0-3.0% on multiple cells. Stealth (Fig. 3): TIP payloads show higher cosine similarity to legitimate tool responses than Fixed or TAP across No Defense, Perplexity Filtering, and Finetuned Detector conditions. Ablation (Table III): removing Defense-Awareness drops ASR to 0.0% across all four victim models when facing a finetuned detector; removing Path Feedback drops ASR on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct from 95% (full design) to 73%. The real-world case study successfully caused both a local Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-1M agent (LM Studio) and GPT-4o inside VS Code's MCP extension to cite an attacker-controlled phishing URL as the 'official source' for weather data, demonstrating the attack against a frontier, safety-aligned commercial model in a live developer tool.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is TIP (Tree-structured Injection for Payloads), a black-box attack framework that automatically generates stealthy, semantically coherent JSON prompt-injection payloads to be embedded in Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool responses. TIP casts payload generation as a tree-structured search: a Branch phase (using a surrogate/attacker LLM, Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct) generates candidate payloads via a 'dual coarse-to-fine' strategy (Level 1: choose Implicit Induction vs. Explicit Control attack framing; Level 2: broad exploration of JSON keys/values when score < 0.5, or stable refinement of values only when score >= 0.5); a Prune phase scores candidates with a Monte Carlo evaluation over M=20 sampled user instructions and randomly selected victim models, keeping only top-K nodes; and an Early-Stop condition halts once a best score exceeds a threshold (0.9 for the Fraud scenario, 0.8 for Data-Steal). Two mechanisms specific to this paper are: (1) path-aware feedback, which feeds the attacker LLM the full ancestor history of high-scoring nodes (not just the last failure) to escape local optima, and (2) tool-response simulation, where the attacker LLM first predicts a plausible benign tool response from the tool's description and then extends/perturbs only its concluding segment, keeping perplexity low relative to legitimate outputs. A defense-aware adaptation module monitors which defense caused a prior failure and steers subsequent generation toward defense-specific evasive variants (e.g., producing summary-resistant payloads if a rewriting defense is detected). The authors also formalize a 'stealthy update' / Supply-Chain Indirect Prompt Injection (SCIPI) threat model in which an MCP tool provider passes initial registration/audit as benign and only later modifies server-side logic to inject payloads into dynamic response fields (leaving tool metadata untouched to avoid re-audits).
threat_model: Attacker is a malicious or later-compromised third-party MCP tool provider (not a webpage/content author as in generic IPI work). Capabilities: full control over the values returned in the tool's JSON response fields (e.g., 'summary', 'notes', error/status fields), but the attacker leaves rigid tool metadata (name, description) untouched to avoid triggering re-registration audits. Constraints: strict black-box access to the victim agent -- no visibility into the victim's system prompt, LLM weights/gradients, or prior chat history; payloads must generalize across unseen instructions and models. Two attacker objectives are evaluated: (1) Social Engineering/Fraud -- get the agent to proactively recommend an attacker-controlled phishing URL; (2) Context Exfiltration/Data Steal -- get the agent to read sensitive context (e.g., tracking numbers, itineraries) and transmit it via a subsequent tool call (e.g., an email send) to an attacker-controlled address. Formal objective: find payload P maximizing E_q~Q[P(a_target | A(q, r_tool ∪ P))] subject to Perplexity(P | r_tool) < delta, i.e., adversarial potency subject to a stealth/coherence constraint, distinguishing this from prior white-box/high-perplexity attacks like GCG.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Discussion, Sec. VIII-B): (1) Optimization latency -- TIP still requires an offline black-box query/training phase (reduced to ~100 queries from TAP's ~2500, but not zero) before a transferable payload can be deployed instantly; (2) the evaluation is single-turn/immediate-injection only, i.e., it measures hijacking the agent in the very next turn after the poisoned tool response, and does not evaluate dormant/multi-turn-persistent injected instructions that trigger only after several subsequent interactions; (3) the defense-aware mechanism is validated against four current defense types and the authors explicitly frame future defenses (e.g., aggressive re-paraphrasing by a separate trusted LLM) as an open 'defensive arms race' not fully addressed here. The paper also devotes a discussion section (VIII-A) to justifying that even reduced ASRs (e.g., 68.2% for ShipManager under Finetuned Detector) constitute meaningful risk at deployment scale, rather than presenting this as a limitation of the attack itself. The case study is explicitly scoped as conducted only on a local, researcher-controlled, isolated environment with no external systems affected. Ethically, note the paper self-discloses that its writing was polished with GPT-4o (proofread by the authors) -- unrelated to methodology but noted for transparency.

datasets_used: InjecAgent benchmark tools (extended): GetWeather, GetProduct, ExpediaBooking, ShipManager
M=20 sampled user instructions per Monte Carlo evaluation round, drawn from a training set
quantitative_results: No Defense ASR: TIP 100.0% (GetProduct), 100.0% (GetWeather), 100.0% (ExpediaBooking), 95.0% (ShipManager) vs Fixed 16.0%/45.0%/10.2%/0.0% and TAP 91.0%/90.0%/0.0%/0.0%
No Defense Query Count: TIP 100/80/20/60 vs TAP 2580/2480/2580/2580 (four tools respectively)
Sandwich Prevention, Fraud/GetProduct: TIP 100.0% ASR vs Fixed 5.0%, TAP 78.6%
Finetuned Detector, ExpediaBooking: TIP 97.8% ASR vs TAP 20.0%, Fixed 0.0%
Perplexity Filtering, GetProduct/GetWeather: TIP 100.0%/100.0% ASR vs TAP 52.5%/87.0%
Transferability (Table II), No Defense, GetWeather, Llama3.3-70B-Instruct: TIP 100.0% vs Fixed 2.0%, TAP 39.0%
Transferability, Instruction Prevention, Data Steal (ShipManager), Llama3.3-70B-Instruct: TIP 92.0% vs Fixed 1.0%, TAP 0.0%
Ablation (Table III), Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: Full Design 95.0% ASR vs w/o Path Feedback 73.0%, w/o Strategy 37.7%, w/o Defense-Awareness 0.0%
Ablation, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct: Full Design 96.0% vs w/o Defense-Awareness 0.0% (finetuned-detector setting)
Early-stop thresholds: 0.9 for Fraud scenario, 0.8 for Data-Steal scenario
baselines_compared: TAP (Tree of Attacks, prior black-box tree-search injection method, without TIP's defense-aware adaptation and using only local rather than path-aware feedback)
